select_when: The value of adoption may rise materially as more users, contributors, or counterparties join the same system.
The team needs to know whether growth mechanics are self-reinforcing after a threshold or are merely linear scale wrapped in better storytelling.
A strategy depends on reaching critical mass before the system becomes viable, defensible, or self-sustaining.
The next move should test density, reach, or seeding assumptions rather than assuming that ordinary growth automatically compounds into network value.
avoid_when: Leaders are labeling ordinary scale benefits as network effects without showing that each additional participant actually increases value for the next one.
The strategy is boiling the ocean by overcomplicating the system before critical mass is even reachable.

curation_notes.summary: Kept the model focused on critical mass, density, self-reinforcing adoption, and anti-fake-network-effects discipline.
donor_drops: Did not preserve broader platform rhetoric because the raw source is strongest on threshold mechanics, feedback loops, and viability tests.
open_questions: The output type compresses a source that also spans seeding strategy and message design for adoption.

select_when.note: The raw source is explicit on value increasing with participation, threshold effects, and deciding whether growth is self-reinforcing.

avoid_when.note: Both anti-activation conditions are grounded in source warnings about fake network-effects claims and overcomplicating the system before viability.

input_type.note: The source clearly activates on threshold and self-reinforcement problems, while the typed input remains a normalized routing label.

output_type.note: The source supports critical-mass testing and feedback assessment; the output label compresses that into a reusable intervention artifact.

reasoning_types.note: Network-effects is strongest as systems and threshold-risk reasoning rather than as generic growth advice.
